summary: Gruber analyzes a New York Times report on the escalating rivalry between Apple and Google, arguing that the conflict is genuine and not media sensationalism. He cites evidence including Apple's near-acquisition of AdMob, heated meetings where Jobs accused Google of stealing iPhone features, and board member Bill Campbell being forced to choose sides. Gruber contends that Jobs genuinely views Google as an existential threat to Apple's core business, leading to aggressive moves like the HTC patent suit (aimed at making Android costly), poaching Google employees, and possibly replacing Google services in Apple products. He frames the situation as a cold war turned shooting war, with both companies now competing directly in mobile computing and entertainment.
key_points: The Apple-Google rivalry has moved beyond normal competitive tension into active hostilities, evidenced by patent lawsuits, employee poaching, and forcing mutual advisors to choose sides
Apple's HTC patent suit is fundamentally about Google — making Android expensive to use by imposing legal defense costs on handset makers
Jobs genuinely believes Google threatens Apple's core business, and that belief alone is enough to drive Apple to war regardless of whether it's objectively true
Apple faces asymmetric decisions because its products depend on Google services (search, maps), while Google has no such dependency on Apple
The Google Voice App Store rejection is best explained as competitive spite rather than any principled policy reason
money_quotes: I think Steve Jobs genuinely sees Google as threatening Apple's core business. It doesn't really matter whether he's right (although the more I consider it, the more I think he is). Jobs believes it, and so Apple is going to war.

That's all about Google — about creating a situation where Android is no longer a free operating system for handset makers in the U.S., because the cost of using it is an expensive legal defense against Apple.
The cold war has ended and the shooting war has begun.

The unofficial partnership Apple and Google forged a decade ago grew during a period when the two companies were focused on very different markets. Now, their sights for the coming decade are on the same markets: mobile computing devices and entertainment.
Patent lawsuits are not usual. Poaching employees is not usual. Forcing a mutual advisor like Bill Campbell to choose sides is not usual.
Apple faces more decisions than Google in such a war, because Apple has products that use Google services; Google doesn't have products that use Apple services.
I'm not sure what to expect next, other than for things to get uglier.
key_insight: The Apple-Google conflict has escalated from competitive tension into genuine corporate warfare, driven by Jobs's conviction that Google threatens Apple's core business and evidenced by patent suits, employee poaching, and forced loyalty tests that go far beyond normal rivalry.
